coredata.dc:title: Functional edible coating based on carbon dots and gelatin/cellulose nanofibers for fresh egg preservation

coredata.dc:description: A nanocomposite formulation based on cellulose nanofibers (CNF)/gelatin(G) blend was developed and added with various concentrations of nitrogen-functionalized carbon dots (NCD) to use as antibacterial coatings for shell eggs. The physicochemical and functional properties of the films were analyzed, and it was found that NCD enhanced the flexibility of the nanocomposite films by forming strong hydrogen bonds due to the surface functional groups. Although the addition of NCD decreased the water vapor barrier properties, the UV-blocking ability of the films was significantly enhanced, achieving more than 90 % UV-blocking effect with minimal effect on film transparency. The nanocomposite films C/G/NCD (Cellulose Nanofibers/Gelatin/NCD) were loaded with different percentages of NCD such as 1,2,3 and 5 %. Among them C/G/NCD5 % exhibited strong antibacterial and antioxidant activities, eradicating 100 % of Staphylococcus aureus and Salmonella enterica within 12 h and exhibited excellent free radical scavenging properties. When coated on raw eggs, the C/G/NCD5 % formulation effectively prevented Salmonella contamination and inhibited further bacterial growth during storage. Our results suggest that the NCD-enhanced CNF/gelatin nanocomposite coating has significant potential as an antimicrobial agent, providing a promising solution for extending the shelf life of eggs and reducing the risk of Salmonella contamination during storage.

head.ce:title: Functional edible coating based on carbon dots and gelatin/cellulose nanofibers for fresh egg preservation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A nanocomposite formulation based on cellulose nanofibers (CNF)/gelatin(G) blend was developed and added with various concentrations of nitrogen-functionalized carbon dots (NCD) to use as antibacterial coatings for shell eggs. The physicochemical and functional properties of the films were analyzed, and it was found that NCD enhanced the flexibility of the nanocomposite films by forming strong hydrogen bonds due to the surface functional groups. Although the addition of NCD decreased the water vapor barrier properties, the UV-blocking ability of the films was significantly enhanced, achieving more than 90 % UV-blocking effect with minimal effect on film transparency. The nanocomposite films C/G/NCD (Cellulose Nanofibers/Gelatin/NCD) were loaded with different percentages of NCD such as 1,2,3 and 5 %. Among them C/G/NCD5 % exhibited strong antibacterial and antioxidant activities, eradicating 100 % of Staphylococcus aureus and Salmonella enterica within 12 h and exhibited excellent free radical scavenging properties. When coated on raw eggs, the C/G/NCD5 % formulation effectively prevented Salmonella contamination and inhibited further bacterial growth during storage. Our results suggest that the NCD-enhanced CNF/gelatin nanocomposite coating has significant potential as an antimicrobial agent, providing a promising solution for extending the shelf life of eggs and reducing the risk of Salmonella contamination during storage.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Eggs are a good source of high-quality protein and other healthy nutrients. For this reason, eggs form an essential component in the diet of most people around the world. In addition, eggs are useful in the food sector due to their functional properties as coagulants, emulsifiers, and foaming agents (Hidas et al., 2021). However, eggs begin to age from the moment they are laid, and the aging process changes their chemical, physical, and functional properties (Rachtanapun et al., 2021). Over time, this process leads to fat oxidation, increased pH, a breakdown of protein structures, and a loss of viscosity. The resulting deformation reduces the foam's stability, changes their coagulation properties, and reduces its water-holding capacity (Vlčková et al., 2019).

Also, the quality of eggs gradually deteriorates during storage, resulting in thinning of the egg white, weakening of the yolk membrane, and decreased yolk viscosity (Hidalgo et al., 2006). Moisture loss and carbon dioxide infiltration through the pores of the eggshell are one of the major causes of quality deterioration (Stadelman, 2017). Not only the storage time, temperature also significantly impacts egg integrity, especially when room temperature is used (Pires et al., 2020). Therefore, fresh eggs should be stored in a cool place at 7 °C or less from the time of production to the time of consumption (Liu et al., 2022). Nevertheless, European Commission Regulation No. 589/2008 states that eggs sold to consumers must not be refrigerated prior to sale (EU, 2017). Additionally, numerous foods have been linked to outbreaks and cases of salmonellosis (Boelaert et al., 2019; Meinen et al., 2019; US CDC, 2024). However, according to EFSA and ECDC (2019), consumption of eggs and egg products was associated with 45.6 % of salmonellosis outbreaks recorded in the EU in 2018 (Boelaert et al., 2019). Additionally, this pathogen-foodborne combination caused most of the 2422 hospitalizations associated with the outbreak in the United States (Dewey-Mattia et al., 2019). A meta-analysis found that consuming raw or soft-boiled eggs may occasionally increase the risk of salmonellosis (Domingues et al., 2012). De Knegt et al. reported that eggs were the main source of strains causing human salmonellosis, accounting for 42.4 % of human infections, and Salmonella Enteritidis was identified as the causative agent in 95.9 % of these cases. The study looked at the source of strains in 24 European countries (De Knegt et al., 2015). According to Ferrari et al., Salmonella enteritidis is the major serotype associated with human salmonellosis, including infections associated with eggs, and has been implicated in recent outbreaks in many countries (Ferrari et al., 2019).

The contents of the whole egg can become contaminated with Salmonella in two different ways: The egg contents are directly contaminated by vertical transmission (ovarian route) before the outer part covers the egg since the hen's reproductive organs are infected with Salmonella (Gantois et al., 2009; Howard et al., 2012). In horizontal transmission, bacteria penetrate through the eggshell. The most important route for Salmonella contamination of eggs is not yet known. However, for S. enterica, the oviductal route of infection appears to be more important than eggshell penetration (Gantois et al., 2009).

Finding effective ways to preserve eggs' internal quality is becoming increasingly popular. A number of products already use edible coatings as accessible technology that controls the oxidation process, gas exchange and humidity (Dhall, 2013). Recent studies have developed coatings made from polysaccharides, starches, oils, proteins, and synthetic polymers to solve these problems (Pires et al., 2020; Rachtanapun et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2017). Coating the pores and cracks in the eggshell strengthens the shell, reduces moisture loss and CO2 emissions, and prevents bacteria from entering the egg from the outside, maintaining its integrity (Falguera et al., 2011). Although mineral oil is now used to coat commercial eggs (Rosser et al., 2011), coatings made from renewable resources that intentionally replace fossil raw materials are gaining increasing attention. This research has greater commercial value, as consumers are likely to be more concerned about food safety, residue-free products, and environmental sustainability. Biopolymers are increasingly being used as coating formulations to preserve a variety of foods (Cazón et al., 2017; Kocira et al., 2021; Nanda et al., 2021; Priyadarshi et al., 2022). Among them, the mixture of proteins and polysaccharides is known to provide synergistic effects between these two types of biopolymers (Roy et al., 2024). Furthermore, various additives, such as natural extracts, essential oils, and sustainable nanomaterials, have been used with biopolymer formulations to impart functional properties such as antioxidant and antimicrobial activities (Priyadarshi & Rhim, 2020). Due to their qualities as a sustainable, affordable, biocompatible, and non-toxic filler in biopolymer formulations, carbon dots (CDs) have recently caught the interest of food packaging experts(Priyadarshi et al., 2024). CDs' size and surface functional groups are a major advantage underpinning the current investigation. Typically, CDs are less than 10 nm in size, and depending on the thickness of the microbial cell wall, they can enter through it. This disturbs the cytoplasmic content by causing an imbalance in the enzymatic reactions that lead to the accumulation of reactive oxygen species. Conversely, the functional groups on the CDs' surface may be interacting electrostatically with the bacterial cell wall and compromising the integrity of the cytoplasmic material inside. These two factors may have contributed to CDs' widespread popularity due to their biocompatibility and antimicrobial efficacy. CDs have also demonstrated the ability to preserve the composition of polymers without altering their structure. Furthermore, CDs enhance the mechanical and physicochemical characteristics of biopolymer compositions because of their characteristics. Based on these reasons CDs were chosen as functional nanofillers of the C/G biopolymeric composition. Furthermore, coating eggs improves their safety and preservability and their appearance, which is one of the main factors influencing consumers' purchasing decisions (Caner, 2005).

Therefore, in the present study, we investigated the improvement of the shelf life of eggs by coating them with gelatin-cellulose nanofiber biopolymer blends with or without nitrogen-functionalized pullulan-modified carbon dots. The effect of the coating formulations on the growth of Salmonella enterica species in eggs was studied at different time intervals. Both coated and uncoated eggs were inoculated with Salmonella enterica species, and the growth of bacterial colonies was monitored at various time intervals. Simultaneously, to characterize the properties of the coating, the carbon dot-loaded gel/CNF biopolymer formulations were cast into free-standing films and their physicochemical and functional properties were evaluated.

Ethylenediamine was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA), and gelatin and cellulose nanofibers were procured from the Korean Bio Polymer Co. Ltd. (Bucheon, Gyeonggi-do, Korea). Citric acid was obtained from the Daejung Chemicals and Metals Co., Ltd, Siheung, Gyeonggi-do, Korea.

Nitrogen-doped CDs (NCDs) were prepared and characterized following the methods described in our previous study (Tammina et al., 2024). 1 g of pullulan (as a capping agent), 5 mL of ethylenediamine (as nitrogen source), and 0.9 g of citric acid (as carbon source), were dissolved in 50 mL of distilled water, then transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined reaction vessel of the hydrothermal reactor and reacted at 180 °C for 6 hours in a muffle furnace. After allowing the reaction solution to cool down to room temperature, the reaction mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 minutes to remove unreacted substances. The brown supernatant was filtered through a Whatman® No. 1 filter paper, followed by a membrane filter (pore size 0.22 μm). The filtrate was lyophilized to obtain a black powder of NCD and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C before further testing and utilization. The detailed characterization of the prepared NCDs and their physicochemical properties have been reported in our previously published work (Tammina et al., 2024).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Free radical scavenging potential of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The DPPH and ABTS free radical scavenging tests were used to assess the antioxidant activity of NCDs. To prepare DPPH solution, 4 mg of DPPH was added to 150 mL of methanol. A range of NCD concentrations (totaling 12.5, 25, 50, 75, and 100 μg/mL) were added to a 10 mL DPPH solution, and the mixture was left to incubate for 30 minutes in the dark. The samples' absorbance was then measured at 517 nm by using a UV-Vis spectrophotometer. A control solution consisted of a test solution devoid of NCD.

For preparing the ABTS solution, potassium persulfate (2.45 mM) and ABTS (7 mM) were mixed with distilled water, which was then left to incubate in the dark for 16 hours. The solution's absorbance was calibrated using distilled water to 1 (a.u.) at the wavelength of 734 nm. A range of NCDs was added to 10 mL of the prepared solution (to create the final concentration of 12.5, 25, 50, 75, and 100 μg/mL). The mixture was subsequently left in the dark for 30 minutes, and the absorbance values at 734 nm were recorded. The control group was the prepared solution devoid of NCDs. The following formula was used to determine the CDs' antioxidant activity:
(1)
Antioxidant activity
%
= [ (
A
o
−
A
T
) /
A
o
] × 100
where, according to the assay, Ao and AT represent the absorbances of the sample and control at their respective wavelengths.

The zone of inhibition and broth microdilution method was used to assess the bactericidal potential of NCDs against common Gram-negative bacteria (Escherichia coli, Salmonella enterica, Pseudomonas aeruginosa) and Gram-positive bacteria (Listeria monocytogenes, Staphylococcus aureus, and Bacillus cereus). These methods revealed the inhibition zone (mm), minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC), and minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of NCDs against the test bacteria samples.

A fresh inoculum was prepared using 20 mL of sterilized peptone water and 10 μL of glycerol-preserved bacterial stock. The inoculum was then incubated at 37 °C for 16 hours. The obtained inoculum solution was diluted further to 106-107 CFU/mL after 16 hours of incubation. 100 μL of the bacterial solution was spread inoculated on solidified agar media in Petri plates. Tryptic soy broth (TSB) and brain heart infusion (BHI) agar media were used as the growth media for Gram-negative and Gram-positive strains, respectively. Following this, wells of 50 μL capacity were bored into the media, and an equal volume of NCD (50 μL) solution was added to each well. The plates were incubated for 16 hours at 37 °C, after which the zone of inhibition was determined in millimeters (mm).

For MIC/MBC determination, various concentrations of NCDs were serially diluted from 1000 µg/mL to 3.9 µg/mL in a 96-well microtiter plate. The experiment was conducted in triplicates, with 30 µL of a bacterial solution (∼106 CFU/mL) and 100 µL of broth added to each well in addition to different NCD concentrations. The prepared plates were stored in the incubator overnight at 37 °C. Afterward, 30 µL of resazurin (a redox indicator, 0.1 mg/mL) was added to each well and again incubated for two hours to observe any color changes. The change in resazurin color from purple to pink indicated the presence of viable bacterial colonies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of gelatin/CNF/NCDs films
ce:section.ce:para: A uniform polymer solution was prepared for each film formulation by mixing 200 mL of distilled water with 1.2 g of the plasticizer glycerol, adding 2 g each of gelatin and CNF on a dry weight basis, and vigorously stirring the mixture for an hour at 90 °C. After the polymers dissolved, the solution was allowed to cool down to 50 °C, and NCDs at different percentages (1 %, 2 %, 3 %, and 5 %) were added to the respective polymer solutions and allowed to mix for 10 minutes. The polymer solution without NCDs was used as a control. The polymer solutions were then cast onto glass plates with a PTFE surface coating and allowed to dry for 48 hours at room temperature (22 °C). After drying, the films were removed and cured for 72 h in a humidity chamber (25 °C temperature and 50 % relative humidity). The generated films were designated as C/G, C/G/NCD1 %, C/G/NCD2 %, C/G/NCD3 %, and C/G/NCD5 %. The chemical analysis of the films was carried out using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, ATR PRO 4X FTIR spectrophotometer, Jasco, Japan). Further, a field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, SU 8010, Hitachi Co., Ltd., S4 Matsuda, Japan) was used for morphological analysis. The details about the characterization parameters have been reported in previous studies (Wagh et al., 2024).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Color and optical properties of films
ce:section.ce:para:
The CR-400 Chromameter (Konica Minolta, Tokyo, Japan) was used to evaluate the color values of the films. A conventional white color plate with the values L* = 97.75, a* = 0.49, and b* = 1.96, which represent lightness, greenness/redness, and blueness/yellowness, respectively. The total color difference (∆E) of the film was determined as follows:
(2)
∆ E =
Δ L
2
+
Δ a
2
+
Δ b
2
where ∆L, ∆a, and ∆b are the differences in color intensity between the reference plate and the film sample. To evaluate the UV barrier and transparency of the films, the percentage light transmittance at 280 nm (T280) and 660 nm (T660) was measured using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Mecasys Optizen POP Series UV/Vis, Seoul, Korea).

The films were cut into specimens of 25 mm by 150 mm using a precision double-blade cutter (Metrotec LB.02/A, Metrotec, SA, San Sebastian, Spain). The film thickness was measured at five randomly selected points on each strip using a digital micrometer (QuantuMike IP 65, Mitutoyo Corporation, Kanagawa, Japan) with a precision of 1 µm, and the average thickness value was determined. The mechanical properties of the films were evaluated utilizing an Instron universal testing instrument (Model 5565, Instron Engineering Corporation, Canton, MA, USA) by the ASTM A882–88 standard procedure. The machine was operated in tensile mode, with an initial gripping distance of 50 mm and a crosshead velocity of 50 mm/min. Tensile strength (TS) and the percentage of elongation at break (EB) of the films were measured.

The film's WVP was evaluated by applying the ASTM E96–95 standard. The gravimetric cup, measuring 68 mm in diameter and 25 mm in depth, containing eighteen milliliters of distilled water, was used for this purpose. The 75 mm × 75 mm film section was placed above the cup and screwed firmly to allow water vapor to pass through the film alone. After properly sealing each cup, its weight was noted, and it was then put in a humidity-controlled chamber (RHFX1077, Jeio Tech Co. Ltd., Ansan, South Korea) that was set to 25 °C and 50 % relative humidity. A variation in cup weight was observed every hour for a maximum of eight hours. Plotting the weight change across time yielded a slope that indicated the films' water vapor transmission rate (WVTR, g/m2.s). The following formulas were used to compute the film WVP (g.m/m2.Pa.s).
(3)
WVP = ( WVTR × L ) / ∆ p
where L is the film thickness (m) and ∆p is the partial vapor pressure difference (Pa) between each side of the film.

An identical procedure, as described in Section 2.2.1, was used to conduct the test. A UV–visible spectrophotometer was used to determine the absorption at 517 and 734 nm, correspondingly, after 50 mg of every film type was added by weight to the ten milliliters of DPPH and ABTS solutions in place of CDs.

The antibacterial properties of the film samples were evaluated against L. monocytogenes and E. coli using the total viable colony count approach. BHI and TSB media were used to prepare sterile bacterial broth cultures of L. monocytogenes and E. coli, respectively, with a final concentration of 105-106 CFU/mL. Following the culture preparation, 20 mL of each bacterial solution was added to 100 mL of disinfected conical flasks, and 100 mg of each film sample was immersed in them. The control was the broth that was devoid of film. The flasks with bacterial culture exposed to the films, along with the control flasks, were subjected to an incubator shaker set at 37 °C temperature and 150 rpm shaker rotation speed. After every 3 h of incubation up to 12 h, 20 μL culture from each flask was inoculated onto agar plates in triplicate, and the total viable colony count for each bacterial strain was estimated. The antimicrobial activity was estimated as the reduction in bacterial colonies, represented as log CFU/mL, compared to the control sample.

Fresh eggs were purchased from a local supermarket in Seoul, South Korea and were divided into 3 experimental groups. The eggs from each group were UV sterilized before further use. The eggs in the first group were left uncoated, while those in the second and third groups were coated with C/G and C/G/NCD5 % coating formulations, respectively. For this, a dip coating method was used. The eggs were dipped in the coating formulation for 2 min, followed by straining of the excess solution and air drying to form a uniform coating on the surface.

For inoculation, Salmonella enterica was cultured in Rappaport-Vassiliadis (RV) broth media. The overnight prepared culture of 5 log CFU/mL was prepared in a beaker, and eggs from each group were inoculated by dipping in it for 15 seconds, followed by air drying in a sterile environment for 30 minutes. The inoculated eggs were stored in an incubation chamber with a set temperature of 25 °C.

The Salmonella colonies on the eggs were counted after every two days for an 8-day storage period. For this, three eggs from each experimental group were taken, and each was transferred to a stomacher bag with 150 mL of peptone water. The eggs were crushed in the bags, and the liquids were allowed to mix with the peptone water homogeneously. After this, 1 mL aliquots of serially diluted homogenate were plated in triplicate on Xylose Lysine Deoxycholate agar (XLD agar) plates, which serve as the selective growth media for Salmonella species. Plates were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h, colonies were counted, and the results were expressed in log CFU/egg.

Every experiment was conducted three times, and the results were presented as mean ± SD. Using SPSS statistical analysis software (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA), a one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) and Duncan's multiple range test were used to determine the significance of each mean value (p < 0.05).

Pullulan-modified NCDs' capacity for radical scavenging was assessed by determining their ability to scavenge DPPH and ABTS free radicals. This assessment is very important for extending the shelf life of food products. Naturally, the presence of scavengers prevents food from spoiling by scavenging radicals and lowering food oxidation. Fig. 1 shows the antioxidant activity of NCDs against DPPH and ABTS free radicals.

DPPH and ABTS solutions were added to different concentrations of NCDs in the range of 12.5–100 μg/mL concentration. With increasing concentrations of NCDs, the DPPH scavenging was increased, and the maximum activity was observed at 100 μg/mL concentration of NCDs with a 48.9 % scavenging of the free radicals. In addition, the ABTS scavenging was also increased tremendously with increasing concentration of NCDs. Interestingly, against ABTS, the maximum scavenging activity (100 %) was observed at a much lower concentration of 50 μg/mL. There could be a solvent-related differential in the free radical scavenging potential of the NCDs (Priyadarshi et al., 2024). Due to its hydrophilic characteristics, NCDs are more active in polar solvents compared to less polar ones. Consequently, NCDs are less active against the DPPH free radicals in a methanol solvent than the ABTS free radicals in an aqueous solution (Tammina & Rhim, 2023a).

In the fundamental carbon structure, certain parameters facilitate the delocalization of electron spins and the complete eradication of free radicals (Ruiz et al., 2017). The radical scavenging activity of NCDs can be enhanced by the inclusion of functional groups, which are primarily obtained from OH, NH2, CC, and C-N groups. By either proton donation or adduct formation following the donation of an electron with delocalization of free electrons, these groups neutralize the free radicals, further stabilizing the sp2 hybridized carbon network (Das et al., 2021).

The antimicrobial activity of NCDs was evaluated via well diffusion and broth microdilution method against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. Fig. 2 shows the bacterial inhibition zone formed by NCDs against pathogenic bacteria E. coli, S. enterica, P. aeruginosa, L. monocytogenes, B. cereus, and S. aureus. The zone diameter was measured in cm and presented in Table 1 . The observed data indicate that NCDs are effective against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. However, the zone size is different from one to another. This inhibition area varies depending on several factors, such as diffusion potential, medium width, and the interaction of NCDs with the pathogen type concerned. Thus, it is difficult to conclude the effectiveness of NCDs against each pathogen type. Still, this experiment revealed NCDs' antibacterial potential against Gram-positive and Gram-negative pathogens. The broth dilution method was utilized to determine the approximate effective concentration of NCDs, and the respective results are presented in Table 1 . It was observed that the NCDs displayed an MIC and MBC value of 250 μg/mL and 500 μg/mL, respectively, against all pathogens. Both well diffusion and broth dilution evaluations revealed the promising antimicrobial potential of NCDs against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. The rich amino, carboxyl, hydroxyl, and carbonyl groups on the surface of NCDs might be responsible for displaying the good antimicrobial properties of NCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological characteristics of films
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of NCD addition on the chemical structure of the CNF/gelatin films was observed with the help of FTIR analysis, and the results are shown in Fig. 3. The films exhibited a broad peak around 3299 cm−1, which was attributed to the O-H stretching vibrations and the intermolecular hydrogen bonding present in the polymers, specifically gelatin and cellulose (Ahmadi et al., 2021). At 2925 cm−1 and 2878 cm−1, peaks were identified as resulting from the C-H stretching of the alkane groups within these polymers (Roy & Rhim, 2022). A peak at 1630 cm−1 was linked to the formation of peptide bonds, which arise from the amine and carbonyl groups in gelatin (Riahi et al., 2021; Roy & Rhim, 2022). The peaks observed at 1544 cm−1 were attributed to the N-H stretching in the amide-II region of proteins, while those at 1452 cm−1 and 1238 cm−1 corresponded to the C-N and N-H stretching vibrations, respectively, in the gelatin structure (Roy & Rhim, 2022). The peak detected at 1336 cm−1 corresponded to the CH2 wagging motion characteristic of cellulose (Roy & Rhim, 2022). Finally, peaks at 1159 and 1031 cm−1 were associated with the stretching of C-C and C-O-C pyranose rings in cellulose, respectively (Roy & Rhim, 2022).

With the addition of NCDs, the key IR peaks characteristic of CNF and Gel were preserved in the nanocomposite films. This similarity in IR absorption is attributed to the consistent chemical environment of these bands, maintaining the same structural morphology due to hydrogen bonding among the functional groups of gelatin (carboxylic, amide, hydroxyl), CNF (hydroxyl), and NCDs (carboxylic, hydroxyl, amide). However, minor variations were observed, which confirm the uniform integration of NCDs in the C/G matrix, and interactions between the additives and polymer matrix. Due to hydrogen bonding between C/G and NCDs, the broad absorption band around 3299 cm−1 (highlighted in yellow) in the pure C/G film corresponding to O-H and N-H stretching vibrations displayed a slight shift in all the NCD-added films (Khan et al., 2023; Konwar et al., 2015; Sul et al., 2025; Xiao et al., 2022). Also, the intensity of this absorption band was observed to have been increased in the nanocomposite films, indicating an increase in the number of hydroxyl and amine functional groups provided by the NCDs to the C/G matrix, confirming the successful and uniform addition of NCDs to C/G films (Chen et al., 2023)(https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbpol.2023.120938). Further, with the increase in NCD concentration, the intensity of the band located at 1402 cm−1 was increased, which might correspond to the O-H bending vibration of carboxylic functional groups (Ezati et al., 2022a) provided by NCDs, confirming their integration into the polymer matrix.

The morphological analysis of neat and NCD-added CNF/gelatin films was carried out using SEM, and the results are shown in Fig. 4. The morphology of the neat C/G film was uniform without any apparent deformities, pores or cracks, indicating a good compatibility between CNF and gelatin. The addition of NCDs did not significantly affect the surface morphology of the films. However, some wrinkling could be observed on the surface, which could be due to the higher moisture absorption by the films due to the hydrophilic nature of NCDs (Priyadarshi et al., 2024). The cross-sectional images of the neat films also displayed an intact morphology devoid of any cracks or deformities. A fine wavy texture was observed, which can be attributed to the fibrous nature of CNF (Sul et al., 2024a). With the increase in NCD concentration, no apparent difference in the cross-sectional morphology was observed up to a concentration of 2 wt%. However, slight enhancement in the wavy texture was observed once the concentration was increased to 3 wt% and higher. Again, this may be due to the NCDs' hydrophilic character and surface hydrophilic functional groups like hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amine. These groups tend to bind moisture, thereby causing an increase in wrinkled appearance. However, both the surface and cross-sectional morphologies indicate a uniform dispersion of NCDs in the polymer matrix and an excellent compatibility between the biopolymers and fillers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Color and optical properties of films
ce:section.ce:para: The color properties of the neat and nanocomposite CNF/gelatin-based films are shown in Table 2. The neat C/G film was transparent and colorless, as depicted by the L* value greater than 90 and the T660 value around 89 %. With the addition of NCDs, the L* value decreased slightly but significantly. On the other hand, the T660 value did not show any significant difference until the NCD concentration reached 3 wt%. For C/G/NCD5 % films, the T660 value was 87.6 %, which was less than a 2 % decrease compared to the control film. The results indicate that despite a decrease in L* and T660 values with the addition of NCDs, composite films were transparent and displayed excellent brightness. However, the b* value, which indicates the yellowness of the films, displayed a continuous and statistically significant increase with an increase in NCD concentrations. The values were increased from 1.2 for the control films to 31.4 for films added with 5 % NCDs.

Along with this, the ∆E * values also increased continuously with an increase in yellowness of the films, reaching a value of 27.6 for the 5 % NCD added films compared to 5.8 for the control films, indicating that the appearance of the nanocomposite films substantially changed compared to the control ones. Overall, the addition of NCDs imparted a yellowish tinge to the films without affecting their brightness and transparency. A similar increase in the yellowness of the films on the addition of carbon dots was observed by other researchers as well, which originates from the naturally brown color of the carbon dots(Ezati et al., 2022b; Riahi et al., 2024b; Sul et al., 2024b; Tammina & Rhim, 2023b).

Besides this, the addition of NCDs also had a significant effect on the UV-blocking properties of the nanocomposite films. It is evident from the T280 values of the films which increased from 40.2 % for the neat films to 2.1 % for the 5 % NCD added films, indicating around 94 % reduction in UV transmission through the films. The light transmission spectra of the films in the UV visible region ( Fig. 5 a) also indicate a significant increase in the UV barrier properties with the addition of NCDs in the films, especially in the UV-A and UV-B regions. Fig. 5 b shows that the neat films were only able to block 36 % of UV-B and 18 % of UV-A radiation. With just a 1 % addition of NCDs, a 70 % blocking of both UV-A and UV-B radiations was observed. As the concentration of NCDs increased further, the UV blocking properties continuously increased, showing a maximum UV-B blocking of around 92 % and UV-A blocking of around 96 %.

The effect of NCD addition on the mechanical properties of the CNF/gelatin-based films was studied, and the results are shown in Table 3. The thickness of the neat C/G films was observed to be 33.8 μm, which was in accordance with the results obtained for CNF blends with other biopolymers (Kim et al., 2023; Riahi et al., 2024a). With the addition of NCDs, the thickness increased in direct relation, which may be due to the increase in solid content, as also observed in previous studies (Purohit et al., 2023). Also, the interchain H-bonding is facilitated by NCDs, which act as small plasticizer entities due to their hydroxyl-rich surface, thereby increasing the interchain distance and, thus, increasing the film thickness (Kashfipour et al., 2019). Besides, the NCDs also bind water molecules due to their hydrophilic nature, which further contributes to the plasticizing effects (Priyadarshi et al., 2024). This plasticizing effect of NCDs was also evident in the mechanical properties of the CNF/gelatin nanocomposite films. With the increase in NCD concentration, a continuous concentration-dependent decrease in tensile strength was observed. For the neat films, a tensile strength of 53 MPa was obtained, which drastically reduced to 24.8 MPa for films added with 1 % w/w NCDs. This value further decreased to only 15 MPa when the NCD concentration was increased to 5 %. The corresponding elastic modulus also decreased from 2.9 GPa to 0.8 GPa.

On the other hand, the elongation at break percentage increased from 9.1 % for the neat C/G films to 17.5 % for films with 5 % NCDs, indicating a two-fold increase in the flexibility of the films. The results clearly indicate the effect of NCDs as plasticizers, which decreased the tensile strength of the films while the elongation at break increased (Eslami et al., 2023). This direct proportionality of elastic modulus and inverse proportionality of elongation at break with tensile strength has been widely reported in the literature, especially for nanomaterials added by polymeric films (Priyadarshi & Negi, 2017).

The WVP values of the neat CNF/gelatin films and those incorporated with different concentrations of NCDs were measured to understand the impact of NCDs on the moisture barrier properties of the films. The WVP values for the different formulations are presented in Table 3. The neat C/G film exhibited a WVP value of 0.78 × 10⁻⁹ g·m/m²·Pa·s, serving as the baseline for comparison. With the incorporation of 1 % NCDs, the WVP slightly increased to 0.83 × 10⁻⁹ g·m/m²·Pa·s, indicating a marginal reduction in the film's moisture barrier capability. As the NCD concentration was increased to 2 %, a more pronounced increase in WVP was observed, reaching 1.05 × 10⁻⁹ g·m/m²·Pa·s. This trend continued with the 3 % NCD concentration, where the WVP value peaked at 1.19 ± 0.16 × 10⁻⁹ g·m/m²·Pa·s. However, when the NCD concentration was further increased to 5 %, the WVP value slightly decreased to 1.02 ± 0.12 × 10⁻⁹ g·m/m²·Pa·s. However, the difference between the WVP values of films with 3 % and 5 % NCDs was not significantly different statistically.

The observed increase in WVP with NCD incorporation can be attributed to the hydrophilic nature of NCDs, which are rich in surface hydroxyl groups resulting from pullulan passivation. These groups can interact with water molecules, thereby enhancing the water vapor permeability of the films. The NCDs likely disrupt the tight packing of the CNF/gelatin matrix due to the formation of H-bonding and acting as a plasticizer, leading to increased free volume within the film structure (Kashfipour et al., 2019). This structural change facilitates the diffusion of water vapor, as also observed from the SEM images.

The antioxidant activity of CNF/gelatin blend films incorporated with varying concentrations of NCDs was evaluated in terms of ABTS and DPPH free radical scavenging potential, as illustrated in Fig. 6. The C/G film, which did not contain any NCDs, exhibited the lowest antioxidant activity. The film was able to scavenge only 9 % of DPPH and 17 % of ABTS free radicals. This result is expected since no additional antioxidant agents were present to enhance the film's ability to scavenge free radicals. The introduction of NCDs into the CNF/gelatin matrix resulted in a significant increase in antioxidant activity, which was found to be concentration-dependent.

At low concentrations of NCDs, i.e., 1 wt%, a modest but noticeable increase in antioxidant activity was observed towards DPPH, scavenging around 25 % of the free radicals present in the solution. In contradiction, at the same concentration of NCDs, a substantial 89 % scavenging of ABTS free radicals was observed. The difference in the ABTS and DPPH scavenging ability of the film stems from the hydrophilic character of the NCDs, which act more proficiently in the aqueous ABTS solution compared to the methanolic solution of DPPH, thus reacting better with ABTS than with DPPH free radicals, thus, neutralizing the former more efficiently (Priyadarshi et al., 2024). It also indicates that even a small amount of NCDs can contribute to the antioxidant properties of the films, likely due to the inherent antioxidant capabilities of NCDs, which are even stronger attributed to their nitrogen-functionalization and the presence of amine functional groups on the surface (Min et al., 2023).

As the concentration of NCDs was increased to a medium level, the antioxidant activity of the films showed a substantial rise. The observed increase can be attributed to the higher availability of antioxidant sites provided by the higher amount of NCDs within the film matrix. While a 100 % scavenging of ABTS free radicals was achieved by 2 wt% NCD concentration in the films, the DPPH scavenging percentage increased to 47 % and further increased in direct relation to the NCD concentration. The most pronounced DPPH scavenging of around 70 % was observed at the highest concentration of NCDs, i.e., 5 wt%. At this concentration, the films exhibited the maximum capacity to neutralize free radicals, suggesting that this formulation could be the most effective for applications requiring high antioxidant activity.

The antimicrobial activity of the nanocomposite films was studied against Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus and Gram-negative Salmonella enterica bacterial strains, and the results are provided in Fig. 7. It was observed that pristine CNF/gelatin films did not show any antimicrobial activity against any of the bacterial strains. Even after a test interval of 12 hours, the antibacterial rate percentage was almost negligible for C/G films against both Staphylococcus aureus and Salmonella enterica. When 1 wt% NCDs were added to the C/G films, the antimicrobial activity slightly increased, and after the test period of 12 h, the antibacterial activity was increased to almost 30 % against both bacterial strains. With further increase of NCD concentrations, the antimicrobial activity of the nanocomposite films was continuously increased. The results indicated a continuous concentration-dependent antimicrobial activity of the NCD-added nanocomposite films, which is a common characteristic observed for nanocomposite films added with varying concentrations of antimicrobial materials (Priyadarshi et al., 2021; Priyadarshi & Negi, 2017). This continuous increase in antimicrobial activity can be attributed to the increased release of nanomaterials from the films when their initial concentration in the films is higher. However, it was observed that the films were more active against Salmonella enterica compared to Staphylococcus aureus, and an almost 90 % antimicrobial rate was observed against Gram-negative pathogen even for C/G/NCD3 % films within 3 h of exposure, increasing to 100 % after 9 h.

On the other hand, for the same films, only a 55 % antimicrobial rate was obtained against the Gram-positive bacteria, increasing to around 90 % after 9 h. Nevertheless, the C/G/NCD5 % films were able to completely eradicate the growth of both the pathogenic bacteria within a three-hour exposure time. This higher antimicrobial activity of the nanocomposite films, especially C/G/NCD5 % films, indicated that this formulation could be a good candidate for application as a coating formulation for raw eggs which are frequently contaminated by Salmonella enterica during storage, causing outbreaks of food poisoning (Pires et al., 2020).

Eggs are perishable food products, and a common method to preserve them during the supply chain is to store them under refrigerated conditions, preferably below 7.2 °C (Pires et al., 2020). However, possible temperature abuse during the supply chain can facilitate the contamination of eggs by foodborne pathogens, such as Salmonella enterica, and promote their growth on the eggshell surface, which eventually serve as vectors for the transmission of these bacterial to humans (Pires et al., 2020). Research also suggests that the viability and growth of Salmonella enterica can be sustained even in refrigerated eggs (Pires et al., 2020). Various cases of Salmonella-related infections have been reported due to the consumption of contaminated eggs. The USA faced a major Salmonella-related outbreak in the 1970s (Pires et al., 2020), while in the European Union, 90 % of the reported foodborne diseases are linked to the consumption of eggs and egg products contaminated with Salmonella species (Eddin et al., 2019).

To address this issue, the C/G/NCD5 % film formulation was applied as coatings on the eggs, and its efficacy in retarding the growth of inoculated Salmonella enterica under storage conditions was studied over 8 days ( Fig. 8). For comparison, the uncoated eggs and those coated with pristine C/G formulation were also analyzed. It was observed that the Salmonella population rapidly multiplied on the uncoated eggs, and the number of colonies after 4 days rose to 5 log CFU/egg, compared to the initial inoculated value of around 2 log CFU/egg. However, for the next 4 days, the population declined, possibly due to the lack of food source for the bacteria on the highly oxygenated and nutrient-poor eggshell surface, which pushed the population into the stationery and death phase after 6 days of storage (Maki et al., 2020). This hypothesis was supported by the continuous growth of bacterial population on the surface of eggs coated with pristine C/G formulation, which did not possess any antimicrobial activity but provided a carbon-rich feed source to sustain the continuous growth and replication of bacteria inoculated on the egg surface. The Salmonella population on the C/G-coated egg continuously increased, reaching around 6.5 CFU/egg at the end of the 8-day test period.

On the other hand, the eggs coated with functional C/G/NCD5 % displayed a 100 % eradication of all bacterial colonies in just 2 days of storage, and no further contamination or infection occurred throughout the storage period. The results indicate the efficacy of the antimicrobial NCD-added nanocomposite formulation as a potential coating material for the long-term preservation of eggs under storage, not only at refrigerated temperatures but also under ambient conditions.

CNF/gelatin-based formulations containing various concentrations of NCD were prepared to develop antimicrobial coating formulations for preventing salmonella contamination and extending the shelf life of eggs during storage. The prepared formulations were cast into films to study their physicochemical and functional properties. Structural and morphological analyses revealed that NCD formed strong hydrogen bonding networks between biopolymer chains due to the presence of surface functional groups such as hydroxyl and amine groups. Due to its hydrophilicity and small size, NCD acted as a plasticizer, which improved the flexibility of the nanocomposite films. In addition, the hydrophilicity of NCD decreased the water vapor barrier properties and water contact angle values of the nanocomposite films.

Nevertheless, the addition of NCD significantly improved the UV-blocking properties of the films in the UV-A and UV-B regions of the UV–visible spectrum, blocking more than 90 % of UV radiation. In addition, this property did not have a significant negative effect on the transparency of the films, and all nanocomposite films had visible light transmittance exceeding 85 %. Moreover, due to NCD's potent antibacterial and antioxidant activities, the resulting nanocomposite films could delay the growth of Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus and Gram-negative Salmonella enterica. The C/G/NCD5 % film eradicated 100 % of the bacterial population in the growth medium within a 12-hour test period. At this NCD concentration, the films also exhibited excellent 100 % ABTS free radical scavenging activity and a significant 70 % neutralization of DPPH free radical.

The C/G/NCD5 % formulation was applied as a coating on the surface of raw eggs. The nanocomposite coating formulation could prevent Salmonella contamination of eggs. Salmonella colonies were observed on the surface of uncoated eggs and eggs coated with pure CNF/gelatin formulation, whereas no pathogenic bacterial colonies were observed on eggs coated with the C/G/NCD5 % formulation even after 2 days of storage. Furthermore, the coating prevented further bacterial contamination of eggs during storage, demonstrating the efficacy of the CNF/gelatin formulation with NCD as an effective antimicrobial coating material for extending the shelf life of eggs.

Jeyakumar Saranya Packialakshmi: Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Jong-Whan Rhim: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.
